symbols: sword in hand — the instrument of deliverance and Judith's role as warrior-heroine
rich garments and jewelry — the beauty Judith used to gain access to Holofernes
determined gaze — the resolve of a woman acting on behalf of her people
dark background — the danger and secrecy of Judith's mission into the enemy camp
description: A three-quarter length portrait of a woman with long dark hair, wearing an elaborate coral-red dress with gold embroidery and a green-gold sash at the waist. Her left shoulder is bare. She wears gold bracelets and earrings. She holds a curved sword at her side, pointing downward. Her expression is serious and resolute, gazing slightly to the side. The background is dark with a suggestion of richly patterned fabric behind her. The overall mood is dramatic and powerful.
theological_description: Constant's Judith depicts the heroine of the deuterocanonical Book of Judith, who saved her people by entering the camp of the Assyrian general Holofernes, charming him with her beauty, and beheading him with his own sword while he lay drunk. Though the Book of Judith is not in the Protestant canon, the story resonates deeply with biblical themes of God using unlikely deliverers. Judith's act parallels Jael's slaying of Sisera (Judges 4:17-22) and Deborah's leadership (Judges 4-5) — women through whom God delivered Israel when male leaders failed. The rich garments recall Esther preparing to approach King Ahasuerus to save her people (Esther 5:1). Judith's courage embodies the principle God declared to Gideon: 'The Lord said to Gideon, The people with you are too many for me to give the Midianites into their hand, lest Israel boast over me, saying, My own hand has saved me' (Judges 7:2). God consistently chooses the weak and unlikely to confound the strong (1 Corinthians 1:27). Judith's song of triumph echoes Hannah's and Mary's canticles — the humble exalted, the proud brought low.

scripture_refs.reason: Jael slaying Sisera — the biblical parallel of a woman delivering Israel by killing an enemy commander
God choosing the weak to shame the strong — Judith as an unlikely instrument of divine deliverance
Esther adorning herself to approach the king and save her people — Judith's parallel use of beauty for deliverance
Deborah's song celebrating Jael — the tradition of women warriors celebrated in Israel's history
